topicData.title: Pizza
topicData.subtitle: Dish · Neapolitan / Italian Cuisine
topicData.summary: The word pizza first appeared in a Latin text in Gaeta, Italy in 997 CE. The modern tomato-based pizza was developed in Naples in the 18th century after tomatoes arrived from the Americas.
topicData.aiInsight: Pizza etymology remains debated. The tomato pizza we know is only 200-300 years old, yet flatbreads with toppings are documented in Ancient Egypt, Greece, and Persia.

nameChanges.name: Flatbread (Ancient)
nameChanges.context: Ancient Greeks and Romans ate focaccia-like flatbreads with oil, cheese, and herbs.

nameChanges.name: Picea / Piza
nameChanges.context: First written record: a Gaeta notary document (997 CE) requiring delivery of twelve pizzas.

nameChanges.name: Pizza Napolitana
nameChanges.context: Tomatoes from Americas adopted by Neapolitan poor in 1700s. Pizza Margherita created for Queen Margherita in 1889.

nameChanges.name: Pizza
nameChanges.context: Italian immigrants took pizza to America. New York and Chicago styles developed.

historicalContext.content: Flatbreads with toppings eaten across Mediterranean.
Street food vendors sold cheap flatbreads.
Tomatoes adopted by Neapolitan poor. Pizza became a cheap staple meal.
Italian immigrants brought pizza to New York. NY slice and deep dish Chicago styles developed.

comments.author: Giovanni Russo
comments.text: Pizza is from Naples. Most people do not know true Neapolitan pizza history!

topicData.title: Google
topicData.subtitle: Company · Technology / Search Engine
topicData.summary: Google began as a Stanford research project called BackRub in 1996. Renamed Google in 1997, the name is a play on googol (10 to the 100th power) — chosen to represent the mission to organise vast quantities of information on the web.
topicData.aiInsight: The name Google was reportedly a misspelling of googol when Larry Page and Sergey Brin were checking domain availability. The error became one of the most valuable brand names in history.

nameChanges.name: BackRub
nameChanges.context: Larry Page and Sergey Brin Stanford research project that ranked web pages by back-link analysis.

nameChanges.name: Google
nameChanges.meaning: Play on googol (10 to the power 100)
nameChanges.context: Named Google September 1997. Company incorporated 4 September 1998.

historicalContext.content: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met at Stanford. BackRub became Google. Incorporated in a Menlo Park garage.
Survived dot-com crash. IPO in 2004. Acquired YouTube (2006), Android (2005).
Restructured as Alphabet. Ventures in AI (DeepMind), autonomous vehicles (Waymo).

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.text: BackRub is such an awkward name. Google was clearly an upgrade!

topicData.title: Mumbai
topicData.subtitle: City · Maharashtra, India
topicData.summary: Mumbai was known as Bombay during British colonial rule. The name Bombay comes from the Portuguese Bom Bahia (Good Bay). In 1995 the Maharashtra government renamed it Mumbai after the goddess Mumba Devi worshipped by the local Koli fishing community.
topicData.aiInsight: Bombay and Mumbai were used simultaneously for years after the 1995 rename. The stock exchange is still called BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange) and has not renamed itself, showing how business identity can outlast political renames.

nameChanges.name: Heptanesia
nameChanges.context: Ancient Greek name for the seven-island archipelago that formed the base of modern Mumbai.

nameChanges.name: Bom Bahia
nameChanges.context: Portuguese took the islands in 1534 from the Sultanate of Gujarat. Named for the natural harbour.

nameChanges.name: Bombay
nameChanges.context: Given to Charles II of England as part of Catherine of Braganza dowry in 1661. Leased to East India Company.

nameChanges.name: Mumbai
nameChanges.context: Maharashtra government renamed on 1 November 1995 to honour goddess Mumba Devi and assert Marathi identity.

historicalContext.content: Seven islands inhabited by Koli fisherfolk. Part of various kingdoms including Silhara and Yadava dynasties.
Portuguese then British colonial rule. Became major trading port and capital of Bombay Presidency.
Capital of Maharashtra state. Renamed Mumbai 1995. India financial capital, Bollywood home.

comments.author: Rahul Mehta
comments.text: The BSE still being called Bombay Stock Exchange shows how business identity outlasts political renames.

topicData.title: Neem Tree
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Azadirachta indica · South Asia
topicData.summary: The Neem tree has been called the village pharmacy in India. Its name derives from Persian nim and Sanskrit nimba, and was known to ancient Indians as Sarva Roga Nivarini — the cure for all ailments.
topicData.aiInsight: Neem naming spans Sanskrit, Persian, Arabic, and modern scientific Latin, each reflecting a different culture. The scientific name Azadirachta comes from Persian azad darakht-i Hind (free tree of India).

nameChanges.name: Nimba
nameChanges.context: Mentioned in Atharva Veda and Ayurvedic texts as medicinal and sacred.

nameChanges.name: Margosa / Indian Lilac
nameChanges.context: British colonial botany recorded it as Margosa tree for its bitter compounds.
nameChanges.source: British East India Company Botanical Records

nameChanges.name: Azadirachta indica
nameChanges.context: Formally classified by A.H.L. de Jussieu in 1830. Used globally in organic pesticides, medicine, and cosmetics.

historicalContext.content: All parts used in Ayurveda: leaves, bark, seeds, oil. Called Sarva Roga Nivarini (all-disease remover).
India fought and won against neem patent attempts in 1990s, calling it biopiracy.

topicData.title: Atom
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Physics / Chemistry
topicData.summary: The word atom comes from the Ancient Greek atomos meaning indivisible or uncuttable. Ironically, the 20th century proved atoms are very much divisible — into protons, neutrons, electrons, and further into quarks. The name has survived despite being scientifically incorrect.
topicData.aiInsight: The atom is one of science great naming ironies — called indivisible in Greek, it was split in 1932. We have kept the wrong name out of historical habit.

nameChanges.name: Atomos
nameChanges.context: Democritus and Leucippus proposed all matter consists of tiny indivisible particles called atomos.

nameChanges.name: Atom (chemical element)
nameChanges.meaning: Smallest unit of a chemical element
nameChanges.context: John Dalton formalised atomic theory in 1808.
nameChanges.source: John Dalton, A New System of Chemical Philosophy, 1808

nameChanges.name: Atom (divisible)
nameChanges.meaning: Nuclear particle made of protons, neutrons, electrons
nameChanges.context: Electron discovered 1897, nucleus 1911, proton 1920, neutron 1932. Atoms proved divisible — name retained.

historicalContext.content: Democritus, Leucippus proposed atomic theory philosophically — without experimental evidence.
Dalton used atomic theory to explain chemical reactions. Set relative atomic masses.
Electron, proton, neutron discovered. Bohr model. Quantum mechanics developed. Nuclear fission achieved 1938.

topicData.title: Istanbul
topicData.subtitle: City · Turkey
topicData.summary: Istanbul is one of the most renamed cities in history, having been called Byzantium, Constantinople, and Istanbul. Capital of the Byzantine Empire then the Ottoman Empire, it was officially renamed Istanbul in 1930 by Ataturk.
topicData.aiInsight: The name Constantinople literally means City of Constantine in Greek (Konstantinos + polis). When Ataturk renamed it Istanbul in 1930, he was actually restoring a colloquial Greek/Turkish name people were already using informally — Istanbul comes from Greek eis tin polin meaning to the city or simply in the city.

nameChanges.name: Byzantium
nameChanges.meaning: Named after founder Byzas of Megara
nameChanges.context: Founded as Greek colony by Byzas. Strategic location at Bosphorus strait.

nameChanges.name: Constantinople
nameChanges.context: Emperor Constantine I rebuilt and renamed it as new Roman capital in 330 CE.

nameChanges.name: Kostantiniyye
nameChanges.context: Mehmed II conquered in 1453. Became Ottoman capital. Turks used Kostantiniyye officially.

nameChanges.name: Istanbul
nameChanges.meaning: From Greek eis tin polin — to the city / in the city
nameChanges.context: Ataturk officially renamed it Istanbul in 1930 as part of broader Turkish modernisation and nationalisation reforms.

historicalContext.content: Greek colony at strategic Bosphorus crossing point between Europe and Asia.
Capital of Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire for 1,123 years. Centre of Orthodox Christianity.
Capital of Ottoman Empire. Mehmed II renamed it Kostantiniyye after conquest.
Turkey established 1923. Capital moved to Ankara. Istanbul renamed 1930 by Ataturk.

comments.author: Mehmet Yilmaz
comments.text: Istanbul being derived from a Greek phrase shows how deeply intertwined the cultures were.

topicData.title: Kolkata
topicData.subtitle: City · West Bengal, India
topicData.summary: Kolkata was known as Calcutta during British colonial rule and served as the capital of British India from 1772 to 1911. The name Calcutta was an Anglicisation of the Bengali Kalikata. It was officially renamed Kolkata in 2001.
topicData.aiInsight: Calcutta was the British Indian Empire capital from 1772 to 1911. Moving the capital to Delhi in 1911 was a deliberate political move by the British after the 1905 Bengal Partition protests. The Kolkata rename in 2001 restored the Bengali pronunciation that locals had always used.

nameChanges.name: Kalikata
nameChanges.meaning: Possibly from Kali ghat (steps of goddess Kali) or Kilkila (flat area)
nameChanges.context: Small village near the Hooghly River. Temple of Kali Ghat nearby.

nameChanges.name: Calcutta
nameChanges.context: Job Charnock established British trading post in 1690. Became capital of British India 1772-1911.

nameChanges.name: Kolkata
nameChanges.context: West Bengal government officially renamed it Kolkata on 1 January 2001.

historicalContext.content: Capital of British India for 139 years. Centre of Bengal Renaissance, Indian independence movement.
Capital of West Bengal. Intellectual and cultural capital of India. Renamed Kolkata 2001.

topicData.title: Beijing
topicData.subtitle: Capital City · China
topicData.summary: Beijing has served as China national capital for most of the last 700 years under multiple dynasties and governments. Its name has changed from Ji to Yanjing to Dadu to Beiping to Beijing, reflecting each ruling power and political era.
topicData.aiInsight: Beijing means Northern Capital in Chinese (bei = north, jing = capital). When the capital was moved south, the city was renamed Beiping (Northern Peace) to reflect it was no longer the capital. When the capital returned, it became Beijing again. Peking is the old Wade-Giles romanisation still used in Peking Duck and Peking Opera.

nameChanges.name: Ji
nameChanges.context: Capital of the state of Yan during the Zhou Dynasty period.

nameChanges.name: Yanjing
nameChanges.context: Liao dynasty capital. Name still used poetically and for a famous local beer brand.

nameChanges.name: Dadu
nameChanges.context: Kublai Khan built entirely new city called Dadu (Mongolian) or Khanbaliq (Great Khan City).

nameChanges.name: Beiping
nameChanges.meaning: Northern Peace (not capital during these periods)
nameChanges.context: Ming founder moved capital to Nanjing, renamed city Beiping. Returned to Beijing when Yongle moved capital north again in 1421.

nameChanges.name: Beijing
nameChanges.context: Current name. Capital of Ming, Qing, and now People Republic of China. Peking is old Western romanisation.

historicalContext.content: Capital of Yan state during Zhou Dynasty. Various kingdoms controlled the area.
Kublai Khan built Dadu (Khanbaliq). Marco Polo visited and described it as the greatest city in the world.
Forbidden City built 1406-1420. Capital of imperial China for most of this period.
Republic of China (1912). Communist victory 1949. Beijing declared capital of PRC on 1 October 1949.

topicData.title: Ketchup
topicData.subtitle: Condiment · Global
topicData.summary: Ketchup has nothing to do with tomatoes in its origin. The word derives from the Chinese Hokkien dialect ke-tsiap, a fermented fish sauce. British sailors brought it from Southeast Asia, and it evolved through many forms before Heinz introduced tomato ketchup in 1876.
topicData.aiInsight: Original ketchup was a fish sauce — unrecognisable from today's tomato version. The transformation from salty/fermented to sweet/tomato took 200 years and multiple countries.

nameChanges.name: Ke-tsiap / Kecap
nameChanges.meaning: Brine of pickled fish or shellfish
nameChanges.context: Fermented fish sauce used in Fujian province and throughout Southeast Asia.

nameChanges.name: Catsup / Catchup
nameChanges.context: British sailors brought Southeast Asian fish sauce home. Early British recipes used anchovies and mushrooms — no tomatoes.

nameChanges.name: Ketchup
nameChanges.context: Heinz recipe standardised ketchup globally. 650 million bottles sold annually.

historicalContext.content: Ke-tsiap was a pungent fermented fish sauce used across Southeast Asia.
British adapted the concept using local ingredients — mushroom ketchup, walnut ketchup, oyster ketchup. No tomatoes.
Americans added tomatoes. Heinz introduced preservative-free tomato ketchup in 1876.

comments.author: Tom Richards
comments.text: Mind-blowing that ketchup started as a fish sauce! The tomato version is less than 200 years old.

topicData.title: Nike
topicData.subtitle: Brand · Athletic Footwear and Apparel
topicData.summary: Nike was originally Blue Ribbon Sports, founded by Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman in 1964 as a US distributor of Japanese Onitsuka Tiger shoes. Renamed Nike in 1971 — after the Greek goddess of victory — just before launching its own shoe line.
topicData.aiInsight: The Nike name was suggested by Jeff Johnson (first employee) reportedly from a dream. The Swoosh logo was designed by Carolyn Davidson for 5. Phil Knight reportedly said he did not love it but it would grow on him — it became the world most recognised logo.

nameChanges.name: Blue Ribbon Sports
nameChanges.context: Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman distributed Onitsuka Tiger shoes from Japan.

nameChanges.name: Nike, Inc.
nameChanges.context: Name suggested by Jeff Johnson. Launched with the Swoosh logo (designed for 5). Just Do It tagline added 1988.

historicalContext.content: Knight sold shoes from his car at track meets. Bowerman experimented with shoe designs using a waffle iron.
Own shoe line launched. Air technology introduced 1979. Michael Jordan signed 1984.
Just Do It campaign 1988. Tiger Woods partnership 1996. 1B revenue today.

topicData.title: Lotus
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Nelumbo nucifera · Sacred Flower of Asia
topicData.summary: The lotus holds the distinction of being sacred to three world religions simultaneously — Hinduism, Buddhism, and ancient Egyptian religion. Its name varies significantly across cultures: Padma in Sanskrit, Nilufar in Persian, Lotus in Greek.
topicData.aiInsight: The lotus has the remarkable property of emerging clean from muddy water — a self-cleaning mechanism now studied by nanotechnology researchers as the Lotus Effect. This physical property inspired its spiritual symbolism of purity across three independent civilisations.

nameChanges.name: Padma / Kamala
nameChanges.context: National flower of India. Symbol of Brahma, Vishnu, Lakshmi, Saraswati. Core Buddhist symbol.

nameChanges.name: Nelumbo nucifera
nameChanges.context: National flower of India and Vietnam.

historicalContext.content: Central symbol in both religions. Deities sit or stand on lotus thrones.
Lotus Effect (self-cleaning) discovered 1970s by Wilhelm Barthlott. Applied to nanotechnology surfaces.
